speaker: Unknown
en: When you're a founder, a CEO, when you have the autonomy to consider, what are the role scopes around me?
zh: 当你是一个创始人，一个 CEO，当你有自主权去考虑，我周围的角色范围是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: Then you can really hire around it.
zh: 那么你真的可以围绕它招聘。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I have some CEOs that I work with who are incredible visionaries, great strategists, really good at managing the board, hiring, et cetera.
zh: 所以我与之合作的一些 CEO 是令人难以置信的远见者，伟大的战略家，非常擅长管理董事会，招聘等等。 [[693]** 非常不擅长管理他们的团队。

speaker: Unknown
en: Terrible at managing their team.
zh: 在管理他们的团队方面很糟糕。

speaker: Unknown
en: They hate it.
zh: 他们讨厌它。

speaker: Unknown
en: So they hire a COO.
zh: 所以他们聘请了一位 COO。

speaker: Unknown
en: And they work in partnership, they have one person who's really internally focused, they get to be externally focused.
zh: 他们合作共事，他们有一个人真正关注内部，他们可以关注外部。

speaker: Unknown
en: That works well, it's a symbiotic relationship.
zh: 这很有效，这是一种共生关系。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you're honest about your strengths and you're weaknesses, then you can start to manage around them.
zh: 如果你对你的优势和劣势诚实，那么你可以开始围绕它们进行管理。

speaker: Unknown
en: This episode is brought to you by Persona, the verified identity platform, helping organizations onboard users, fight fraud and build trust.
zh: 这一集由 Persona 带给你，经过验证的身份平台，帮助组织入职用户，打击欺诈并建立信任。

speaker: Unknown
en: We talk a lot on this podcast about the amazing advances in AI.
zh: 我们在这个播客上谈论了很多关于 AI 的惊人进展。

speaker: Unknown
en: But this can be a double-edged sword.
zh: 但这可能是一把双刃剑。

speaker: Unknown
en: For every wow moment, there are fraudsters using the same tech to wreak havoc.
zh: 每一个令人惊叹的时刻，都有欺诈者利用同样的技术来制造破坏。

speaker: Unknown
en: Laundering money, taking over employee identities and impersonating businesses.
zh: 洗钱，接管员工身份以及冒充企业。

speaker: Unknown
en: Persona helps combat these threats with automated user, business and employee verification.
zh: Persona 帮助利用自动化的用户、企业和员工验证来对抗这些威胁。

speaker: Unknown
en: Whether you're looking to catch candidate fraud, meet age restrictions or keep your platform safe, Persona helps you verify users in a way that's tailored to your specific needs.
zh: 无论你是想发现候选人欺诈、满足年龄限制还是保持平台安全，Persona 都能帮你以适合你特定需求的方式验证用户。

speaker: Unknown
en: Best of all, Persona makes it easy to know who you're dealing with without adding friction for good users.
zh: 最重要的是，Persona 让你很容易知道你在和谁打交道，而不会给优质用户增加摩擦。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is why leading platforms like Etsy, LinkedIn, Square and Lyft trust Persona to secure their platform.
zh: 这就是为什么像 Etsy、LinkedIn、Square 和 Lyft 这样的领先平台信任 Persona 来保护他们平台安全的原因。

speaker: Unknown
en: Persona is also offering my listeners 500 free services per month for one full year.
zh: Persona 还为我的听众提供每月 500 次免费服务，长达一整年。

speaker: Unknown
en: Just head to WithPersona.com/Lenny to get started.
zh: 只需访问 WithPersona.com/Lenny 即可开始。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's WithPersona.
zh: 那就是 WithPersona。

speaker: Unknown
en: com/Lenny.
zh: com/Lenny。

speaker: Unknown
en: Thanks again to Persona for sponsoring this episode.
zh: 再次感谢 Persona 赞助本集节目。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's really nice to know what you want to do and understand what your dream life looks like.
zh: 知道你想做什么，并理解你梦想的生活是什么样的，这真的很棒。

speaker: Unknown
en: You also have a job, you have manager, you got things to do, you got responsibilities.
zh: 你也有一份工作，你有一个经理，你有事情要做，你有责任。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I guess first of all, as you have seen people that are not founders actually make a change to do the things ... to spend more time on their gifts, to actually not just be like ... like there is ... you have agency to move in a direction that'll make you happier.
zh: 所以我想首先，正如你所见，那些不是创始人的人实际上做出了改变去做这些事情……把更多时间花在他们的天赋上，而不仅仅是像……就像有……你有主观能动性朝着能让你更快乐的方向前进。

speaker: Rachel Lockett
en: Absolutely.
zh: 绝对的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Is kind of an implied piece of this.
zh: 这是这其中隐含的一部分。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, I think sometimes people hire a Chief of Staff to help them and compliment them.
zh: 是的，我认为有时人们会聘请一位幕僚长来帮助他们并以此作为补充。

speaker: Unknown
en: Sometimes people design their team with strengths and gifts that they don't have.
zh: 有时人们根据自己的优势和自身所不具备的天赋来设计他们的团队。

speaker: Unknown
en: So it's really ... you can get creative once you really understand, oh, these things give me a ton of energy and these things are exhausting.
zh: 所以这真的……一旦你真正理解了，哦，这些事情给了我很多能量，而这些事情让人精疲力竭，你就可以发挥创意了。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I still need to fill this need for the business.
zh: 但我仍然需要满足业务上的这种需求。

speaker: Unknown
en: What are all the ways I can do it?
zh: 我有哪些方法可以做到这一点？

speaker: Lenny
en: And telling your manager, I think, is such a simple and important part of this. Telling them-
zh: 而且告诉你的经理，我认为，是这其中非常简单且重要的一部分。告诉他们——

speaker: Unknown
en: Absolutely.
zh: 绝对的。

speaker: Unknown
en: ... here's where I want to go, here's the things I want to get better at, here's the things that give me energy.
zh: ……这是我想去的地方，这是我想变得更好的事情，这些是给我带来能量的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: Can we just try to make as much of my role that?
zh: 我们能不能试着让我的工作角色尽可能多地变成那样？

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Especially if you're executing well, people want to retain you.
zh: 特别是如果你执行得很好，人们想留住你。

speaker: Unknown
en: They want to know, what's going to keep you here for the next five years?
zh: 他们想知道，什么能让你在未来五年留在这里？

speaker: Unknown
en: And typically they think that means moving up the ladder.
zh: 通常他们认为这意味着在晋升阶梯上向上爬。

speaker: Unknown
en: But maybe it doesn't for you.
zh: 但也许对你来说不是。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think it does take the courage to move horizontally sometimes to get into your strengths.
zh: 我认为确实需要勇气有时横向移动，以进入你的优势领域。

speaker: Unknown
en: I mean, I've moved horizontally a number of times and I love what I do, I feel like I'm in my natural gifts.
zh: 我是说，我已经横向移动了很多次，而且我热爱我所做的事情，我觉得我在运用我的天然天赋。

speaker: Unknown
en: But it took me a few risks and some uncomfortable jobs that didn't feel like they were worthy of my experience in order to get there.
zh: 但但这需要我冒几次险，并做一些不舒服的工作，这些工作感觉不值得我的经验，但我才到了那里。

speaker: Unknown
en: What's a good percentage of your work life that should be in gifts and things that energize you versus, okay, I actually got [inaudible 00:52:48] stuff.
zh: 你的工作生活中有多大比例应该处于天赋和给你带来能量的事情，而不是，好吧，实际上我得 [听不清 00:52:48] 的事情。

speaker: Rachel Lockett
en: My litmus test is 80%, that's the goal.
zh: 我的试金石是 80%，这就是目标。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's the aspiration.
zh: 那是愿望。

speaker: Unknown
en: You're always going to have 20% of things you don't love doing.
zh: 你总是会有 20% 你不喜欢做的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's just the logistics of getting into the zone that you need to be in.
zh: 只是进入你需要进入的状态所需要的一些后勤工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I really try to push people to think aspirationally, that if you're 80% of the time in your gifts, how much energy you have to give to the world.
zh: 但我真的试图推动人们要有抱负地思考，如果你 80% 的时间都在你的天赋中，你能给世界多少能量。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's so much more inspiring.
zh: 这要有启发性得多。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I want to tell you why I'm passionate about this topic because it actually is how I ended up as an Executive Coach.
zh: 所以我想告诉你为什么我对这个话题充满热情，因为这实际上就是我成为一名高管教练的原因。

speaker: Unknown
en: So 10 years ago, I was working at a small company called Remind, and I was running the UX Research team.
zh: 所以 10 年前，我在一家叫 Remind 的小公司工作，我负责 UX 研究团队。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the CEO asked me to move into the Product Manager role for the Core Product team.
zh: CEO 让我调动到核心产品团队担任产品经理的角色。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I was excited for the opportunity.
zh: 我对这个机会感到兴奋。

speaker: Unknown
en: I had non-technical background, but I thought, "Hey, all these strategists are up there creating the roadmap.
zh: 我没有技术背景，但我认为，“嘿，所有这些战略家都在那里制定路线图。

speaker: Unknown
en: I can do that.
zh: 我也能做那个。

speaker: Unknown
en: I know exactly what our users need."
zh: 我确切地知道我们的用户需要什么。”

speaker: Unknown
en: So I was excited for this.
zh: 所以我对这件事感到很兴奋。

speaker: Unknown
en: I came into the team, there was I think 12 senior engineers, very opinionated, very skeptical, this non-technical PM, but we worked together.
zh: 我进入这个团队时，我想有 12 位高级工程师，非常有主见，非常多疑，我这个非技术产品的产品经理，但我们在一起工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: And what I did was I listened.
zh: 而我所做的是我倾听。

speaker: Unknown
en: I learned what do our users need?
zh: 我了解了我们的用户需要什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: What does this team need?
zh: 这个团队需要什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: What's working and not working?
zh: 什么有效，什么无效？

speaker: Unknown
en: And with-
zh: 并且随着—

speaker: Unknown
en: What does this team need?
zh: 这个团队需要什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: What's working and not working?
zh: 什么有效，什么无效？

speaker: Unknown
en: And within a month, this team was working well together.
zh: 在一个月内，这个团队协作得很好。

speaker: Unknown
en: They were reviewing each other's code base.
zh: 他们在互相审查代码库。

speaker: Unknown
en: They were really disagreeing in a healthy way in our team meetings.
zh: 他们在我们的团队会议上以健康的方式真正地提出异议。

speaker: Unknown
en: They felt more connected to users.
zh: 他们感觉与用户的联系更紧密了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I felt like, "Okay, this rhythm is working."
zh: 我感觉，"好吧，这种节奏行得通。"

speaker: Unknown
en: But what I was also doing as I was at home stressing in the middle of the night about the new user experience, I couldn't decide which of the designs to go with.
zh: 但我也在做的另一件事是，我在半夜在家为新的用户体验感到压力，我无法决定采用哪种设计。

speaker: Unknown
en: I was always over leveraging our data scientists, and I found myself swirling on decisions that didn't need to have so much stress involved.
zh: 我总是过度利用我们的数据科学家，而且我发现自己陷入了一些不需要这么大压力的决定中。

speaker: Unknown
en: And one day I went for a walk with my colleague Zach Abrams, and he was a great product manager and he was listening to me ask all these questions about how to sell the vision of what this product would look like in the future.
zh: 有一天我和我的同事 Zach Abrams 去散步，他是一位伟大的产品经理，他在听我问所有关于如何推销这个产品未来愿景样貌的问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: And he said, "Rachel, you're a zone of genius or your gift is not being a product strategist, but I've watched you over the last few months, and you have gotten the team more motivated than I ever could, and you've influenced the entire executive team behind your ideas, and that's impressive.
zh: 他说，"Rachel，你的天才区域或者说你的天赋不是做产品战略家，但在过去几个月里，我观察到你让团队的积极性比我所能做到的更高，而且你影响了整个高管团队支持你的想法，这令人印象深刻。

speaker: Unknown
en: You're a people person."
zh: 你是一个人缘好的人。"

speaker: Unknown
en: And at first I was offended.
zh: 起初我很被冒犯。

speaker: Unknown
en: What?
zh: 什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: You think I don't have the ability to be a great product leader?
zh: 你认为我没有能力成为一名伟大的产品领导者吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: And yet I sat with what he said, and I knew he was right.
zh: 然而我思考了他所说的话，我知道他是对的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Both my parents are therapists.
zh: 我的父母都是治疗师。

speaker: Unknown
en: I never wanted to be a therapist.
zh: 我从不想成为一名治疗师。

speaker: Unknown
en: Here I am.
zh: 现在我在这里。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm basically a work therapist.
zh: 我基本上就是一名工作治疗师。

speaker: Unknown
en: I love entrepreneurial energy, I love big vision, but I'm a people person.
zh: 我热爱创业的能量，我热爱宏大的愿景，但我是一个人缘好的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I left that, and I realized I love what my coach does.
zh: 然后我离开了那个，我意识到我喜欢我的教练所做的工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: I got trained as a coach.
zh: 我接受培训成为了一名教练。

speaker: Unknown
en: I went into HR leadership.
zh: 我进入了人力资源领导岗位。

speaker: Unknown
en: And Zach, who was a gifted product strategist, went on to lead product at Coinbase and BREX and most recently Bridge, which was acquired by Stripe, and he's still my client.
zh: 而 Zach，这位天赋异禀的产品战略家，后来去领导 Coinbase 和 BREX 的产品，最近在 Bridge，该公司被 Stripe 收购了，他仍然是我的客户。

speaker: Unknown
en: And we've watched our journeys over the last decade, and we've both been honing our gifts.
zh: 在过去十年里，我们看着彼此的旅程，我们都在磨练我们的天赋。

speaker: Unknown
en: Life is more fun when you're in your gifts, and you have more inspiration and capacity to offer the world.
zh: 当你在发挥你的天赋时，生活会更有趣，而且你有更多的灵感和能力去提供给这个世界。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I just want to share that story because it's helpful to be honest with the people you care about when they seem energized and when they seem depleted because sometimes it's a wakeup call for people to really think about what is their spark and to protect it and to feed it.
zh: 所以我想分享这个故事，因为对你关心的人诚实是有帮助的，当他们看起来充满活力时以及当他们看起来精疲力竭时，因为有时这能唤醒人们真正思考他们的火花是什么，并去保护和滋养它。

speaker: Unknown
en: I love that story because I think most people when they hear this advice and this topic of, "Okay, I am feeling depleted, I'm feeling burnt out," I feel like most people jump to, "Okay, but I can't actually do anything about it.
zh: 我喜欢这个故事，因为我认为大多数人当他们听到这个建议和这个话题，"好吧，我感到精疲力竭，我感到倦怠，" 我觉得大多数人会直接跳到，"好吧，但我实际上对此做不了任何事。

speaker: Unknown
en: I have a job.
zh: 我有一份工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: I got responsibilities."
zh: 我有责任。"

speaker: Unknown
en: What I'm getting from this is the most important step is jump to figure out what you actually should be doing.
zh: 我从这中得到的是，最重要的一步是跳出来弄清楚你实际上应该做什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: What gives you energy?
zh: 什么给你能量？

speaker: Unknown
en: What your gifts are.
zh: 你的天赋是什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: It feels like that's the biggest gap for people because once you know that, there are ways to do that.
zh: 感觉这似乎是人们最大的差距，因为一旦你知道了那个，就有方法去做到。

speaker: Unknown
en: Talk to your manager.
zh: 和你的经理谈谈。

speaker: Unknown
en: "It may not be possible today, but here's where I want to be going.
zh: "也许今天不可能，但这是我想要去的方向。

speaker: Unknown
en: Here's what I want to be spending time on."
zh: 这是我想要花时间做的事情。"

speaker: Unknown
en: I love your point you made though about you actually have to be doing well for your manager to listen to you.
zh: 我喜欢你刚才提出的观点，就是你实际上必须表现得好，你的经理才会听你的。

speaker: Unknown
en: You can't just be sucking and then like, "Oh, but I want to work on strategy."
zh: 你不能表现得很糟糕，然后说，"哦，但我想要做战略。"

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, it's no one else's job to help you live in your gifts.
zh: 嗯，帮助你活在自己的天赋里不是其他任何人的工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: And what I notice in big companies is people are often annoyed or frustrated with their management for not making their job more interesting.
zh: 我在大公司里注意到的是，人们经常因为管理层没有让他们的工作变得更有趣而感到恼火或沮丧。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's like, "No, your manager's job is to help you perform in the job you were hired to do.
zh: 这就像，“不，你经理的工作是帮助你在你受聘从事的工作中表现出色。”

speaker: Unknown
en: It's your job to navigate your career."
zh: “你的工作则是规划你的职业生涯。”

speaker: Unknown
en: So over the arc of your career, how do you match your gifts with the world's needs?
zh: 所以在你整个职业生涯的跨度中，你如何将你的天赋与世界需求相匹配？

speaker: Unknown
en: And if the world right now is your company, how do you understand the needs enough so that you can apply your strengths to those needs?
zh: 如果现在的世界就是你的公司，你如何足够理解这些需求，以便你能将你的优势应用到这些需求上？

speaker: Unknown
en: This reminds me, there's a couple of guests I've had on the podcast who did this.
zh: 这让我想起，我播客上有过几位嘉宾是这样做的。

speaker: Unknown
en: They're both founders, so this is specific to founders, but Rahul Vohra at Superhuman, he realized he's not the best executor operations person, so he hired a president that took all that office plate.
zh: 他们都是创始人，所以这是针对创始人的，但 Superhuman 的 Rahul Vohra 意识到他不是最好的执行运营人员，所以他聘请了一位总裁来接管所有的办公室杂务。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then Darmesh, co-founder of HubSpot, he knew from the beginning he didn't want to manage people, so he made a rule with his co-founder, "I will never have reports."
zh: 然后 HubSpot 的联合创始人 Darmesh，他从一开始就知道他不想管理人，所以他和他的联合创始人制定了一个规则，“我永远不会拥有下属。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And he's the CTO, I believe, and has zero reports, has no one-on-ones.
zh: 我相信他是 CTO，拥有零下属，没有一对一会议。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that it's a beautiful thing to recognize that, but then to actually address the needs of those reports also.
zh: 我认为认识到这一点是一件美好的事情，但然后也要切实解决那些下属的需求。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think often people know, "Ugh, I don't want to have one-on-ones," but just not having anyone to manage those people is not going to be healthy for your company.
zh: 我认为通常人们知道，“唉，我不想进行一对一会议，”但仅仅没有人去管理这些人员对你的公司来说是不健康的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So you have to both take your strength seriously and actively manage around your weaknesses.
zh: 所以你必须既认真对待你的优势，又积极管理你的弱点。

speaker: Unknown
en: Is there any maybe last piece of advice on this topic of helping people get to a place where they're feeling much less depleted and just more energized at work?
zh: 关于这个帮助人们到达一个让他们感到不那么耗尽而在工作中更有精力的话题，你还有什么最后的建议吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: I would start small.
zh: 我会从小处着手。

speaker: Unknown
en: You don't have to leave your job and redesign your life.
zh: 你不必辞职并重新设计你的生活。

speaker: Unknown
en: You can stop going to the optional things that are exhausting.
zh: 你可以停止参加那些令人精疲力竭的可选活动。

speaker: Unknown
en: You can leave space between the things that are depleting that you have to go to outside and go on a 30-minute walk and refuel your tank.
zh: 你可以在那些你必须参加的令人消耗的事情之间留出空间，出去走走 30 分钟并给你的油箱加油。

speaker: Unknown
en: Start with tomorrow.
zh: 从明天开始。

speaker: Unknown
en: What are the three things you're going to do to plug up that gas leak and re-energize your spark?
zh: 你要做哪三件事来堵住那个燃气泄漏并重新点燃你的火花？

speaker: Unknown
en: It might even be you love to read and you're going to start reading 30 minutes before you go to sleep every night.
zh: 甚至可能是你喜欢阅读，你打算在每晚睡觉前开始阅读 30 分钟。

speaker: Unknown
en: It doesn't have to be a dramatic life change, but recognize that only you know what is resonant and what is depleting, and it's your job to take that seriously if you want to show up purposeful and impactful in the world.
zh: 这不必是剧烈的生活改变，但要认识到只有你知道什么是共鸣的，什么是消耗的，如果你想在世界上有目的和有影响力地出现，认真对待它是你的工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: I love that advice.
zh: 我喜欢那个建议。

speaker: Unknown
en: I've actually started reading before bed for 30 minutes, and that's been so joyful, like a physical book with a little nightlight.
zh: 实际上我已经开始在睡前阅读 30 分钟，那是如此快乐，就像一本带小夜灯的实体书。

speaker: Unknown
en: I agree.
zh: 我同意。

speaker: Unknown
en: I love a physical book.
zh: 我喜欢实体书。

speaker: Unknown
en: I have a Kindle, I got all the things, but a physical book on the couch is the best.
zh: 我有一个 Kindle，我拥有所有这些东西，但沙发上的实体书是最好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, it's just that nightlight is key because sometimes at nighttime you need a night book.
zh: 是的，只是那个夜灯是关键，因为有时在夜间你需要一本夜书。

speaker: Unknown
en: So we've been talking mostly so far about individual improvement, how to figure out what you should be working on, just helping learning to coach, things like that.
zh: 所以到目前为止我们主要谈论的是个人提升，如何弄清楚你应该做什么，只是帮助学习教练之类的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: I want to take us up a little level above and talk about team skills, how people get better at working with other people.
zh: 我想让我们稍微提高一个层次，谈谈团队技能，人们如何更好地与他人合作。

speaker: Unknown
en: Something that you are in many ways known for is helping co-founders build better relationships.
zh: 你在很多方面以帮助联合创始人建立更好关系而闻名。

speaker: Unknown
en: And in my experience one of the most challenging parts of starting a company is the co-founder relationship.
zh: 根据我的经验，创业公司最具挑战性的部分之一是联合创始人关系。

speaker: Unknown
en: A lot of people don't realize what they're getting into.
zh: 很多人没有意识到他们正在陷入什么境地。

speaker: Unknown
en: You're basically getting married to this person in a very high stress situation, and you sometimes don't know much about them.
zh: 你基本上是在与这个人结婚，处于一种非常高压力的情况下，而且你有时并不太了解他们。

speaker: Unknown
en: Exactly.
zh: 没错。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then you not working well together is just a huge issue because that all trickles down, and everyone sees it.
zh: 然后你们不能很好地合作是一个巨大的问题，因为所有这一切都会渗透下来，每个人都看得到。

speaker: Unknown
en: And when co-founders leave, it's really bad for everyone.
zh: 当联合创始人离开时，这对每个人都是非常糟糕的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So let me just ask you this, what have you found most helpful in helping co-founders build great relationships, stay happy and productive?
zh: 所以让我问你这个问题，你发现什么最有助于帮助联合创始人建立伟大的关系，保持快乐和高效？

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, thanks for asking this.
zh: 是的，谢谢你问这个问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: I love working with co-founders because I think your core values as a person come out when they interact with someone else's core values.
zh: 我喜欢与联合创始人合作，因为我认为作为一个人，你的核心价值观在与其他人的核心价值观互动时会显现出来。

speaker: Unknown
en: Conflict, healthy conflict or otherwise, is actually where your core values come out.
zh: 冲突，健康的冲突或其他的冲突，实际上是你的核心价值观显现的地方。

speaker: Unknown
en: So it's fascinating to watch people try to do something incredibly hard in the context of someone else's vision, someone else's strengths and weaknesses and navigate that together.
zh: 所以看着人们尝试在另一个人愿景、另一个人的优势和弱点的背景下做一件极其困难的事情并共同驾驭这一点，这是迷人的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So there's so much energy in the co-founder dynamic for me and for co-founders themselves.
zh: 所以对我来说，对联合创始人自己来说，联合创始人动态中有如此多的能量。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's actually something that people don't feel comfortable going to their board about or talking to that many people about because it's a private matter.
zh: 实际上，这是人们对向董事会讨论或与很多人谈论的事情感到不舒服的事情，因为这是一个私事。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's almost like in a marriage you go see a couples' therapist, but you don't tell all your friends that you can't stand your partner, but it keeps you up at night.
zh: 这几乎就像在婚姻中你去见夫妻治疗师，但你不会告诉你所有的朋友你无法忍受你的伴侣，但这让你彻夜难眠。

speaker: Unknown
en: So it's a really tender, important relationship, and there aren't enough supports for co-founders to navigate it.
zh: 所以这是一个非常敏感、重要的关系，而且没有足够的支持来帮助联合创始人驾驭它。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's very normal.
zh: 这很正常。

speaker: Unknown
en: In fact, I know you probably know this stat, 65% of startups fail because of co-founder conflict, and co-founders are in a moment where they're trying to build the future for their business, but also trying to build their own livelihood.
zh: 事实上，我知道你可能知道这个统计数据，65% 的创业公司因为联合创始人冲突而失败，联合创始人在试图为他们业务建立未来的时刻，但也试图建立他们自己的生计。

speaker: Unknown
en: So there's so much at stake to get along with your co-founder.
zh: 所以与你的联合创始人相处有如此多的利害关系。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think the core at its core, what you need in a healthy relationship is, one, self-awareness.
zh: 我认为核心的核心，你在一段健康关系中需要的是，第一，自我意识。

speaker: Unknown
en: What do I bring into this dynamic, and how am I being experienced by the other person?
zh: 我为这种动态带来了什么，以及我是如何被另一个人体验的？

speaker: Unknown
en: What does this other person bring into the dynamic, and how am I reacting to that?
zh: 这另一个人给这种互动带来了什么，我又对此作何反应？

speaker: Unknown
en: So the first is just collective awareness about what is our dynamic.
zh: 所以第一点就是对我们之间的互动有一种集体意识。

speaker: Unknown
en: I like to use the Enneagram for this, but there's all kinds of tools, self-awareness tools that you can use to give a common language to what is my thing and what is your thing.
zh: 我喜欢用九型人格来做这个，但有各种各样的工具、自我认知工具你可以用来给「我的是什么」和「你的是什么」提供一种通用语言。

speaker: Unknown
en: A very classic one has to do with roles.
zh: 一个非常典型的例子与角色有关。

speaker: Unknown
en: CTOs tend to be skeptics.
zh: CTO 往往是怀疑论者。

speaker: Unknown
en: They love facts, they seek knowledge.
zh: 他们热爱事实，他们寻求知识。

speaker: Unknown
en: They want depth of awareness and understanding, and they also like to be self-sufficient.
zh: 他们想要认知和理解的深度，他们也喜欢自给自足。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is a total generalization, but I've seen this pattern over and over again.
zh: 这完全是一种概括，但我已经一次又一次地看到这种模式。

speaker: Unknown
en: The CEO is the person who had to sell the vision.
zh: CEO 是那个必须推销愿景的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: They're a person who loves big picture vision strategy.
zh: 他们是一个热爱大局愿景战略的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: They often are great at influencing others.
zh: 他们通常很擅长影响他人。

speaker: Unknown
en: They love to sell ahead of the reality of what the company's actually built.
zh: 他们喜欢在公司的实际构建现实之前进行推销。

speaker: Unknown
en: This creates an inherent tension between blind optimism and skepticism, and it's a dance that these two roles play together.
zh: 这在盲目乐观和怀疑主义之间产生了内在的张力，这是这两个角色共同演绎的一场舞蹈。

speaker: Unknown
en: So the first part is knowing the dance you're in, so you're not just stepping on each other's toes blindly.
zh: 所以第一部分是知道你处于什么样的舞蹈中，这样你就不会只是盲目地踩到彼此的脚。

speaker: Unknown
en: The second step is actually being conscious about the commitment you're making to your relationship.
zh: 第二步实际上是要有意识地对你对这段关系的承诺。

speaker: Unknown
en: In a marriage, for example, I talk about co-founder vows and recommitments and renewals because in a marriage you get married, and a lot of times people build a family and then they think, "Oh, the relationship will just continue around each other all the time.
zh: 例如在婚姻中，我谈到联合创始人誓言和重新承诺与更新，因为在婚姻中你结婚了，很多时候人们组建了一个家庭，然后他们认为，“哦，这种关系会就这样一直围绕着彼此持续下去。”

speaker: Unknown
en: We're doing this thing together."
zh: “我们要一起做这件事。”

speaker: Unknown
en: But just like couples need a date night, co-founders need time and space to connect with each other to come together and say, "How's this working for you?
zh: 但就像情侣需要一个约会之夜一样，联合创始人的需要时间和空间来彼此联系，聚在一起说，“这对你来说效果如何？”

speaker: Unknown
en: Are we still aligned on our vision and our strategy?
zh: 我们在愿景和战略上仍然保持一致吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: How are we working together?
zh: 我们合作得怎么样？

speaker: Unknown
en: What am I doing that's pissing you off?
zh: 我做了什么让你恼火的事？

speaker: Unknown
en: What are the things that have gone unsaid and that we need to talk about?"
zh: 有哪些未被提及的事情是我们需要谈论的？”

speaker: Unknown
en: But if you're just in the hustle and bustle of running and scaling your startup, you don't make time for that conversation.
zh: 但如果你只是处于运营和扩大初创公司的喧嚣中，你就不会腾出时间进行那次谈话。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think it's incredibly important for co- founders to make space for their relationship, whether that's a dinner every other week, whether that's going out to lunch regularly, whether that's just touching base business-wise, but having an in-person quarterly check-in.
zh: 所以我认为对于联合创始人来说，为他们的关系腾出空间非常重要，无论是每隔一周共进一次晚餐，还是定期出去吃午餐，或者只是在业务上保持联系，但要有面对面的季度检查。

speaker: Unknown
en: That space is critical for the health of a co-founder relationship.
zh: 这个空间对于联合创始人关系的健康至关重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: On that second piece, the vows idea is such a good idea.
zh: 关于第二点，誓言这个主意是个很好的主意。

speaker: Unknown
en: Is that something you actually recommend, here's what I vow to do?
zh: 你真的推荐这样做吗，就是我发誓要做什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, here's what I commit to do.
zh: 是的，这就是我承诺要做的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So recently I actually wrote an article with First Round, and we created a document to help co-founders think about what to integrate into their check-in.
zh: 所以最近我实际上为 First Round 写了一篇文章，我们创建了一份文件来帮助联合创始人思考将什么整合到他们的检查中。

speaker: Unknown
en: So we put out weekly check-in, monthly check-in and annual and just questions to sit down and ask each other.
zh: 所以我们发布了每周检查、每月检查和每年检查，只是坐下来互相询问的问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: Active listening skills will come in handy in those conversations, but it's about taking space out of hustling and running the business to think about the business from a like to say, instead of being on the dance floor, you need time on the balcony to look down at what's happening.
zh: 主动倾听技巧会在这些对话中派上用场，但这是关于从奔波和经营业务中腾出空间来从……打个比方说，你需要时间在阳台上俯瞰正在发生的事情，而不是在舞池上。

speaker: Unknown
en: How are we doing?
zh: 我们做得怎么样？

speaker: Unknown
en: Is this still working for both of us?
zh: 这对我们俩仍然有效吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: And the vows are really about, how are we going to be together?
zh: 誓言实际上是关于，我们要如何在一起？

speaker: Unknown
en: How are we going to show up?
zh: 我们要如何表现？

speaker: Unknown
en: What's our culture that we're creating?
zh: 我们正在创造的文化是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: Even if you don't want to go through a whole culture exercise early on in building your company, you should have some sense of how you want to show up for each other.
zh: 即使你不想在建立公司的早期经历整个文化练习，你也应该对你们想如何为彼此表现有一些概念。

speaker: Unknown
en: How are we going to make decisions?
zh: 我们要如何做决策？

speaker: Unknown
en: How are we going to deal with conflict?
zh: 我们要如何处理冲突？

speaker: Unknown
en: These are things you can go into intentionally and design with your co-founder.
zh: 这些事情你可以有意地与你的联合创始人一起进入并设计。

speaker: Unknown
en: Awesome.
zh: 太棒了。

speaker: Unknown
en: We're going to link to that post The first step, Enneagram sounds like that's what you recommend, and this is basically a personality profile that a lot of people love.
zh: 我们会链接到那篇文章 第一步，九型人格听起来这就是你推荐的，这基本上是一个很多人都喜欢的人格概况。

speaker: Unknown
en: I really like the Enneagram.
zh: 我真的很喜欢九型人格。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think you can also simply tell each other, "Here are my strengths.
zh: 我认为你也可以简单地告诉对方，“这是我的优势。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Here's what I see as my weaknesses, and what do you think?
zh: “这是我所看到的我的弱点，你怎么看？”

speaker: Unknown
en: Give me some feedback.
zh: “给我一些反馈。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Do you agree?"
zh: “你同意吗？”

speaker: Unknown
en: And you can do that with each other without any personality assessment.
zh: 你可以在没有任何性格评估的情况下互相这样做。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you want to just be scrappy and have an open connected conversation about, you could even say, what are the gifts I bring and the weaknesses I have, and how will I cover those?
zh: 如果你想只是不拘小节地进行一场开放、有联系的对话，你甚至可以说，我带来了什么天赋以及我有什么弱点，我将如何弥补这些？

speaker: Unknown
en: How will I lean into my gifts?
zh: 我将如何发挥我的天赋？

speaker: Unknown
en: How will I cover my weaknesses, and how will you?
zh: 我将如何弥补我的弱点，而你又将如何弥补？

speaker: Unknown
en: And then I think it's worthwhile having a conversation about, what are the gaps neither of us cover that we're likely going to need as we build this business?
zh: 然后我认为值得讨论一下，有哪些空白是我们双方都没有覆盖到的，而在我们建立这项业务时很可能会需要的？

speaker: Unknown
en: What do you recommend people do when they are just like, "Our relationship isn't working great.
zh: 当人们觉得“我们的关系运作得不太好”时，你建议他们怎么做？

speaker: Unknown
en: There's a lot of tension"?
zh: “有很多紧张气氛”？

speaker: Unknown
en: All this advice we've been talking about, at the beginning, here's things you can do to set things up for success.
zh: 我们讨论过的所有这些建议，在开始阶段，都是你可以做的事情，以便为成功做好准备。

speaker: Unknown
en: Understand what you're good at, what you're bringing to the table.
zh: 了解你擅长什么，你能为大局做出什么贡献。

speaker: Unknown
en: Consciously commit to, here's what I'm going to do, here's what you're going to do.
zh: 有意识地承诺，这就是我要做的，那就是你要做的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Have these dinners or lunches.
zh: 参加这些晚餐或午餐。

speaker: Unknown
en: I love this metaphor of going out on the balcony and just reflecting on how it's going.
zh: 我很喜欢这个比喻，就像走到阳台上，只是反思进展如何。

speaker: Unknown
en: So that's all really great.
zh: 所以这一切都非常棒。

speaker: Unknown
en: What if you're just already in it and it's really annoying, I don't like this person that much or so much tension constantly.
zh: 如果你已经身在其中，而且真的很烦人，我不太喜欢这个人，或者持续有太多的紧张气氛，那该怎么办？

speaker: Unknown
en: What are a couple things they can do this week, next week?
zh: 这周、下周他们可以做几件什么事？

speaker: Unknown
en: Co-founders typically come to me either in this early phase where they want to intentionally build something great and they want to set it up for success.
zh: 联合创始人通常在这个早期阶段来找我，他们想有意识地建立一些伟大的东西，并想为成功做好准备。

speaker: Unknown
en: More often co-founders come when they're really frustrated with one another.
zh: 更常见的情况是，联合创始人是在彼此非常沮丧的时候来找我的。

speaker: Unknown
en: They feel the tension is palpable.
zh: 他们感觉到紧张气氛是显而易见的。

speaker: Unknown
en: They can't stand it anymore, but they're still really deeply committed to the business so they don't see an out.
zh: 他们再也无法忍受了，但他们仍然深深地致力于这项业务，所以他们看不到退路。

speaker: Unknown
en: And they knew that at some point they really loved this cofounder, so they see a possibility of recovering, and that's why they want to go get a coach.
zh: 而且他们知道在某个点上他们曾经真的很爱这位联合创始人，所以他们看到了恢复的可能性，这就是为什么他们想去找教练。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm going to give you an example of this PR duo running a fierce business scaling really fast.
zh: 我给你举个例子，有一对 PR 领域的双人组正在运营一项残酷的业务，扩张非常快。

speaker: Unknown
en: And at one point when they started, you had the visionary who was great at selling business.
zh: 在开始的时候，有一位是远见者，非常擅长推销业务。

speaker: Unknown
en: They were both incredible with PR, and the partner was incredible operationally.
zh: 他们两个在公关方面都不可思议，而这位合作伙伴在运营方面不可思议。

speaker: Unknown
en: So as the business scaled, one took on a lot more business development and the other took on all the internal things, but was exhausted by all this people management and all of the elements of running a scaled team that she didn't expect to have to do.
zh: 所以随着业务的扩张，一个人承担了更多的业务发展，另一个人承担了所有的内部事务，但却对所有的人员管理和运营一个扩张团队的所有要素感到筋疲力尽，而这些是她没想到自己必须做的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And when they came, I think both of them weren't sure, can we figure this out?
zh: 当他们来找我时，我想他们两个都不确定，我们能解决这个问题吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Do we want to just sell this thing?
zh: 我们是想把这就卖了算了？

speaker: Unknown
en: Do we want to keep going?
zh: 还是想继续下去？

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think someone said, "End it or send," was what one co-founder said to me.
zh: 我想有人说，“结束它或者滚蛋”，这就是一位联合创始人对我说的。

speaker: Unknown
en: They're coming at this decision point.
zh: 他们正处在这个决策点上。

speaker: Unknown
en: And what I saw them do is, one, they named current state really well.
zh: 而我看到他们做的是，第一，他们很好地命名了当前状态。

speaker: Unknown
en: They were both able to share.
zh: 他们两个都能够分享。

speaker: Unknown
en: We did use a 360.
zh: 我们确实使用了 360 度评估。

speaker: Unknown
en: So they got feedback from their teams and then shared it with one another, but they were able to be open and vulnerable in what was working and what wasn't working.
zh: 所以他们从团队那里得到了反馈，然后彼此分享，但他们能够在什么是有效的、什么是无效的方面保持开放和脆弱。

speaker: Unknown
en: Not immediately, but over time.
zh: 不是立即，而是随着时间推移。

speaker: Unknown
en: And they realized they used to love being partners in this work, but as they began to lead different teams, they grew very distant from one another.
zh: 他们意识到他们曾经喜欢成为这项工作的合作伙伴，但随着他们开始领导不同的团队，他们彼此变得非常疏远。

speaker: Unknown
en: They were living on opposite sides of the country and actually just coming together and realizing what each other was missing and how lonely it is to lead this scaling company without each other's support and how they actually needed the counterbalance to their strengths and didn't have it was a important start to their healing.
zh: 他们住在国家的两端，实际上只是聚在一起，意识到彼此错过了什么，在没有彼此支持的情况下领导这家扩张中的公司是多么孤独，以及他们实际上是多么需要对自己优势的制衡，而却没有这种制衡，这对于他们的治愈是一个重要的开始。

speaker: Unknown
en: And over our coaching, they turned back towards each other and they created more of a rhythm of how they would get together without me involved.
zh: 在我们的辅导过程中，他们重新转向彼此，并建立了一种更多的节奏来安排他们在没有我参与的情况下聚会。

speaker: Unknown
en: And they ended coaching after our arc feeling renewed and really recommitted.
zh: 在我们的辅导周期结束后，他们结束了辅导，感到焕然一新并真正重新投入了。

speaker: Unknown
en: They made some changes on their leadership team to fill their gaps.
zh: 他们对领导团队做了一些改变来填补他们的空白。

speaker: Unknown
en: They also started, I think, meeting once a week virtually, and they started a cadence of getting together in person quarterly.
zh: 此外，我想他们开始每周虚拟见面一次，并开始每季度亲自见面的节奏。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I don't mean to say that just that time means you're going to heal, sometimes coming together and really grappling.
zh: 我并不是说仅仅这段时间就意味着你会治愈，有时聚在一起并真正搏斗。

speaker: Unknown
en: I had one last week where we all came together, we had a great full day in-person discussion about how they were making this co-founder duo, how they were making decisions.
zh: 上周我有一例，我们所有人都聚在一起，我们进行了一整天很棒的面对面讨论，讨论他们如何组建这个联合创始人双人组，他们如何做决策。

speaker: Unknown
en: And after that conversation, it was really clear that one of the co-founders was unhappy and didn't appreciate the other one and was not going to change and realized he was a big part of the problem and I think is going to leave the business.
zh: 那次谈话后，很明显一位联合创始人不快乐，不感激另一位，并且不打算改变，并且意识到他是很大一部分问题，我想他要离开这家公司了。

speaker: Unknown
en: But that's still success because it's clarity.
zh: 但这仍然是成功，因为这是清晰。

speaker: Unknown
en: You're not muddling in the dark, frustrated, unconscious about the interpersonal dynamics you're in, you're making a choice based on your strengths and what the business needs and this relationship dynamic that you're in to either be in it or to lovingly step out of it.
zh: 你不是在黑暗中摸索，感到沮丧，对你所处的人际动态毫无意识，你正在根据你的优势、业务的需求以及你所在的这种关系动态，做出选择，要么参与其中，要么充满爱意地退出。

speaker: Unknown
en: I love how similar this is to just the marriage, all this stuff.
zh: 我很喜欢这与婚姻是多么相似，所有这些事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is the same sort of thing you would do.
zh: 这就是你会做的同样的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: Totally.
zh: 完全。

speaker: Unknown
en: I mean a marriage, you're building a life with a partner.
zh: 我是说婚姻，你是在与伴侣建立生活。

speaker: Unknown
en: So the only difference is a marriage is rooted in sexual attraction and love, and that's not the case always in the co-founder dynamic.
zh: 所以唯一的区别是，婚姻植根于性吸引力和爱，而在联合创始人的动态中并不总是这种情况。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I have worked with couples who are also co-founders, but there should be some element of love for your co-founder.
zh: 但我也曾与既是夫妇也是联合创始人的夫妻合作过，但对你的联合创始人应该有一些爱的成分。

speaker: Unknown
en: In fact, I think that when you work closely with colleagues and you really are able to see their gifts and enable them, you can't help but love them.
zh: 事实上，我认为当你与同事紧密合作，并且真正能够看到他们的天赋并赋能他们时，你会忍不住爱他们。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's a big statement.
zh: 这是一个很宏大的声明。

speaker: Unknown
en: The other takeaway here is that just get coaching.
zh: 这里的另一个要点是，去寻求辅导。

speaker: Unknown
en: It feels like that's the solution if things are just not working great.
zh: 如果事情进展得不太顺利，感觉这就是解决方案。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's only so far you can get just talking.
zh: 光靠谈话能取得的进展是有限的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It takes an evolved facilitator, one of the co-founders, being able to hold space for both their frustration and their empathy in a dynamic that is challenging.
zh: 这需要一位进阶的引导者，其中一位联合创始人，能够在这种充满挑战的动态中，既包容他们的挫败感，也包容他们的共情。

speaker: Unknown
en: So outside support is useful.
zh: 所以外部的支持是有用的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Sometimes it's actually a team member, it's an HR leader.
zh: 有时这实际上是一位团队成员，一位人力资源领导者。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's the GC who happens to have great people skills.
zh: 是那位恰好拥有极强人际交往技巧的总法律顾问。

speaker: Unknown
en: You don't always need a coach, but you need space to be vulnerable, open, and curious.
zh: 你并不总是需要一位教练，但你需要空间来展示脆弱、保持开放和充满好奇。

speaker: Unknown
en: So if you can create that on your own, that's great.
zh: 所以如果你能自己创造这样的空间，那就太好了。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think it's definitely possible.
zh: 我认为这绝对是可能的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Outside of the co-founder relationship, do you have any just tactical tips for people to improve their interpersonal skills with just team members, anyone they work with, just people that may struggle like, "Man, I have a hard time with this person.
zh: 除了联合创始人关系之外，你有没有什么实用的技巧给人们，用来提升他们与普通团队成员、任何共事者的人际交往技巧，或者给那些可能正在挣扎的人，比如，“天哪，我和这个人很难相处。

speaker: Unknown
en: I just have a hard time with a lot of people"?
zh: 我就是和很多人很难相处”？

speaker: Unknown
en: First of all, people when they want to have a conflict or they want to engage in something that's not working, they come in armored and ready to prove their point.
zh: 首先，当人们想要发生冲突，或者想要介入一件进展不顺的事情时，他们总是全副武装、准备证明自己的观点。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's natural.
zh: 这很自然。

speaker: Unknown
en: You've been thinking about this, perseverating over whether you should mention it.
zh: 你一直在思考这件事，反复纠结是否应该提出来。

speaker: Unknown
en: You finally get to the point of engaging, and often there's a misguided view that the goal is to convince the other person that what they're doing is wrong.
zh: 你终于到了要介入的那一步，但通常会有一种误导性的观点，认为目标是让对方相信他们正在做的事情是错的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Actually, the goal of any conflict is to create mutual understanding.
zh: 实际上，任何冲突的目标都是创造相互理解。

speaker: Unknown
en: So when I go in to have a conversation with, let's say my husband who's not doing his share of the parenting, my goal is to help him understand what I'm struggling with so that he can empathize, see clearly what's happening, and perhaps meet my needs in some way.
zh: 所以当我去进行谈话时，比方说和我的丈夫，他没有尽到他那份做父母的责任，我的目标是让他理解我正在挣扎的是什么，这样他就能产生共情，看清正在发生的事情，并或许能在某种程度上满足我的需求。

speaker: Unknown
en: But it's not for me to prove to him how little he's doing in the house because he might have a totally different story about what's happening.
zh: 但这并不是为了我要向他证明他在家里做得多么少，因为他对正在发生的事情可能有一个完全不同的说法。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I'm going to give you a framework that I like that many of my clients use.
zh: 所以我要给你一个我很喜欢的框架，我的许多客户都在使用它。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's from Marshall Rosenberg's Nonviolent Communication.
zh: 它来自马歇尔·卢森堡的非暴力沟通。

speaker: Unknown
en: It is a book and a framework.
zh: 它是一本书，也是一个框架。

speaker: Unknown
en: So it's four steps.
zh: 所以它分为四个步骤。

speaker: Unknown
en: The first step is observations.
zh: 第一步是观察。

speaker: Unknown
en: So my job is to note what is happening factually.
zh: 所以我的工作是实事求是地记录正在发生的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: For example, I noticed that in the last three sprint planning meetings, you didn't invite me to those conversations or share with me the roadmap.
zh: 例如，我注意到在最近的三次冲刺规划会议中，你没有邀请我参加那些对话，也没有和我分享路线图。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's an observation.
zh: 这是一个观察。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's a fact.
zh: 这是一个事实。

speaker: Unknown
en: I could take a picture of it, and no one would argue with it.
zh: 我可以拍一张照片，没人能反驳它。

speaker: Unknown
en: The next step is feelings.
zh: 下一步是感受。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I'm going to express my feelings without blame.
zh: 所以我要表达我的感受而不进行指责。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I felt anxious not knowing what was on the roadmap for the week.
zh: 所以因为不知道本周的路线图上有什么，我感到焦虑。

speaker: Unknown
en: I felt confused about whether that meeting happened or not because I wasn't included.
zh: 因为没被包含在内，我对那次会议是否召开感到困惑。

speaker: Unknown
en: So this is me sharing my feelings so the other party can empathize and understand what I'm going through without being defensive.
zh: 所以这是我在分享我的感受，以便对方能够共情并理解我正在经历什么，而不会产生防御心理。

speaker: Unknown
en: The third step is needs.
zh: 第三步是需求。

speaker: Unknown
en: What are my universal human needs related to this topic?
zh: 我与这个话题相关的普遍人类需求是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: We all have needs.
zh: 我们都有需求。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is not requiring anything of the other person, just helping them understand my needs that are not met.
zh: 这不是向对方提出任何要求，只是帮助他们理解我未被满足的需求。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I have a need for clarity, I have a need for collaboration, I have a need for connection, whatever that is.
zh: 所以我有清晰度的需求，我有协作的需求，我有连接的需求，无论那是什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: And lastly, the step is to make a request.
zh: 最后，一步是提出请求。

speaker: Unknown
en: Now, in this model, the request is an olive branch to help the other person meet you and see you.
zh: 现在，在这个模型中，请求是一种橄榄枝，用来帮助对方回应你、看见你。

speaker: Unknown
en: It shouldn't be something that's impossible to do.
zh: 它不应该是某种不可能做到的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: It should be actually something quite small and easy to achieve for the other person to feel successful in connecting to you and understanding you.
zh: 它实际上应该是一件相当小且容易实现的事情，以便对方在与你建立联系和理解你时感到成功。

speaker: Unknown
en: So in this case, I might make a request.
zh: 所以在这个例子中，我可能会提出一个请求。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'd like to ask you next time you have a sprint planning meeting to include me as optional or to send me the roadmap afterwards that you align on.
zh: 我想请求你下次有冲刺规划会议时，把我作为可选人员包含在内，或者之后把你达成一致的路线图发给我。

speaker: Unknown
en: Now, the other person doesn't have to meet my request.
zh: 当然，对方不必答应我的请求。

speaker: Unknown
en: They might make a counter proposal, but the most important thing of this model in this conversation is that the other person understands what I'm going through and they don't feel reactive so that we can have a mutual conversation about what's going on.
zh: 他们可能会提出反建议，但在这个模型和这次对话中，最重要的一点是，对方理解我正在经历什么，并且他们不感到反应过激，这样我们就可以就正在发生的事情进行一场相互的对话。

speaker: Unknown
en: Wow.
zh: 哇。

speaker: Unknown
en: This point about how when you're trying to convince someone of something, when something is going wrong, this point that your goal is not to convince them, that your goal is to have mutual understanding, that just blew my mind, and I think it's going to change my life.
zh: 关于当你试图说服某人某事，或者当出问题时，你的目标不是说服他们，你的目标是拥有相互理解，这一点太让我震撼了，我认为这将改变我的生活。

speaker: Unknown
en: Wow.
zh: 哇。

speaker: Unknown
en: Lenny, try this with your wife tonight.
zh: Lenny，今晚和你妻子试试这个。

speaker: Unknown
en: NVC is a powerful tool, and actually it's very akin to most models that are about connection.
zh: NVC 是一个强大的工具，实际上它非常类似于大多数关于连接的模型。

speaker: Unknown
en: The Stanford Business School course that has a T, it's called Touchy Feely that everybody loves.
zh: 斯坦福商学院有一门带有 T 的课程，大家都爱死它了，叫 Touchy Feely。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, we've had Carol on the podcast.
zh: 是的，我们请过 Carol 来上播客。

speaker: Unknown
en: Great.
zh: 太棒了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, so Carol Robbins created this movement.
zh: 是的，所以 Carol Robbins 创造了这个运动。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's lit.
zh: 这里有很多。

speaker: Unknown
en: A lot of founders go to her model that's for founders.
zh: 很多创始人去上她为创始人开设的模型课。

speaker: Unknown
en: And tech.
zh: 还有科技界。

speaker: Unknown
en: And this is all about, they talk about a net that you can talk about your feelings and your reaction, but as soon as you cross the net to blaming someone else or making an assumption, they're going to have a defensive response.
zh: 这全都是关于他们谈论的一张网，你可以谈论你的感受和你的反应，但只要你越过这张网去责怪别人或做出假设，他们就会产生防御性反应。

speaker: Unknown
en: But you can be incredibly bold and brave if you stay on your side of the net.
zh: 但如果你留在你这一侧的网内，你可以表现得非常大胆和勇敢。

speaker: Unknown
en: So this model helps you do that because it's really about sharing your emotions and your needs and making a request without blame.
zh: 所以这个模型帮助你做到这一点，因为它实际上是关于分享你的情绪和你的需求，并在没有指责的情况下提出请求。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So what I was going to say as you were going through this framework is here's me, here's what I saw, here's what I'm feeling, here's what I need.
zh: 所以我想说的是，当你经历这个框架时，这就是我，这就是我看到的，这就是我感受到的，这就是我需要的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then now that you have that in context, here's something I'm asking for versus you did this and you're feeling this and you thought this.
zh: 然后既然你有那个背景，这是我在请求的事情，而不是你做了这个，你感觉这个，你认为这个。

speaker: Unknown
en: Exactly.
zh: 没错。

speaker: Unknown
en: It also acknowledges that professionals have feelings.
zh: 它也承认专业人士是有感情的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think that we operate in tech.
zh: 我认为我们在科技行业工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: We're supposed to give all of ourselves, all of our time, all of our energy to this endeavor.
zh: 我们应该把我们自己、我们所有的时间、我们所有的精力都献给这项事业。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's purely logical.
zh: 而且这纯粹是逻辑上的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's not at all true.
zh: 这完全不是真的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's completely emotional.
zh: 这完全是情感上的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if we ignore our feelings, they will bubble up, and we will be unconsciously acting from them.
zh: 如果我们忽略我们的感受，它们会冒出来，我们会无意识地根据它们行动。

speaker: Unknown
en: And there's this implicit power here that if the person cares about you and loves you or values your relationship, knowing that this makes them feel bad will make them want to change.
zh: 这里有一种隐含的力量，如果那个人在乎你、爱你或重视你们的关系，知道这会让他们感觉糟糕会让他们想要改变。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's not like you need to tell them, "Change this thing.
zh: 这并不像你需要告诉他们，“改变这件事。

speaker: Unknown
en: Oh, I didn't realize this made you upset or that you have this need.
zh: 噢，我没意识到这让你不安或者你有这个需求。

speaker: Unknown
en: And now that I know that, okay, now I see why this is important to you."
zh: 既然我知道了，好吧，现在我明白为什么这对你很重要了。”

speaker: Unknown
en: That's exactly right.
zh: 完全正确。

speaker: Unknown
en: And sometimes the other person will hear that and have a different story or a different perspective.
zh: 有时对方听到那话会有不同的故事或不同的看法。

speaker: Unknown
en: So they might say, " Okay, I can honor that request," or, "I hear that request, and I hear your feelings, but let me explain what happened for me."
zh: 所以他们可能会说，“好的，我可以答应这个请求”，或者，“我听到了这个请求，我也听到了你的感受，但让我解释一下我这边发生了什么。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And one way you could do that is, are you open to hearing that?
zh: 做到这一点的一种方式是，你愿意听听那个吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: So they're able to share their side too.
zh: 这样他们也能分享他们那边的情况。

speaker: Unknown
en: You don't have to just agree with the person's request.
zh: 你不必只是同意这个人的请求。

speaker: Unknown
en: As long as you're setting this tone, the other person's more likely to contribute in a way that achieves mutual understanding because once you're vulnerable, they're going to share their vulnerability.
zh: 只要你设定了这个基调，对方就更可能以一种实现相互理解的方式做出贡献，因为一旦你表现出了脆弱，他们就会分享他们的脆弱。

speaker: Unknown
en: Let me remind folks of the framework.
zh: 让我提醒大家这个框架。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm going to try using this.
zh: 我要试着用一下这个。

speaker: Unknown
en: I wish it was a handy acronym off.
zh: 我希望它有一个方便的首字母缩写。

speaker: Unknown
en: So the framework is share what you've observed, just the facts of what is happening.
zh: 所以框架是分享你观察到的，只是正在发生的事实。

speaker: Unknown
en: Just simply, I saw you didn't close the fridge fully.
zh: 就简单地，我看到你没有完全关上冰箱。

speaker: Unknown
en: Your feelings of how that made you feel, the needs that your core human need that drives that feeling, I imagine.
zh: 你的感受，那让你感觉如何，驱动那种感受的核心人类需求，我想象。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then the request you have of the person.
zh: 然后你对这个人的请求。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yes.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I want to make one note I forgot to say, which is feelings are emotions.
zh: 我想做一点备注，我忘了说，就是感受是情绪。

speaker: Unknown
en: So sometimes people say things like, "I feel like you're being a jerk."
zh: 所以有时人们会说这样的话，“我觉得你在像个混蛋。”

speaker: Unknown
en: That's not a feeling, obviously.
zh: 那显然不是一种感受。

speaker: Unknown
en: A feeling is a sensation in your body that results in an emotion.
zh: 感受是你身体里的一种产生情绪的感觉。

speaker: Unknown
en: So naming a feeling is actually not easy for technical leaders sometimes.
zh: 所以对于技术领导者来说，命名一种感受有时并不容易。

speaker: Unknown
en: I want to make that point because emotions are what get you to the underlying humanity of connection.
zh: 我想提出这一点，因为情绪是让你达到连接的基本人性的关键。

speaker: Unknown
en: Emotions are the key to soliciting empathy.
zh: 情绪是引发同理心的关键。

speaker: Unknown
en: Are there phrases that are just examples of non-feelings, like using the word "you" in the way you describe a feeling probably is not a good sign?
zh: 有没有哪些短语只是非感受的例子，比如在你描述一种感受时使用“你”这个词大概不是个好迹象？

speaker: Unknown
en: Exactly.
zh: 没错。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you can say, "I feel like... " even if you add like or, "I feel that... " you're probably going to add a fact.
zh: 如果你能说，“我觉得……”即使你加了 like 或者“我认为……”，你大概会添加一个事实。

speaker: Unknown
en: It should be an emotion word after I feel.
zh: 在我觉得后面应该是一个情绪词。

speaker: Unknown
en: So don't say like, don't say you, don't say that?
zh: 所以不要说 like，不要说 you，不要说 that？

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, exactly.
zh: 对，没错。

speaker: Unknown
en: Awesome.
zh: 太棒了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Along this topic, I chatted with a number of clients that have worked with you over the years, and one of the most common themes that they said you help them with is having difficult conversations.
zh: 沿着这个话题，我和曾与你合作过的许多客户聊过，他们说你在帮助他们进行的对话中最常见的主题之一就是进行艰难的对话。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think we covered actually much of this in what we just talked about, but I'm curious if there's any other advice you have for helping people have difficult conversations.
zh: 我想我们刚才实际上已经涵盖了很多这方面的内容，但我好奇你对于帮助人们进行艰难的对话是否还有其他建议。

speaker: Unknown
en: Let me read a quote from one of your clients.
zh: 让我读一段你的一位客户的引言。

speaker: Unknown
en: Oh, wow.
zh: 哦，哇。

speaker: Unknown
en: I love this.
zh: 我喜欢这个。

speaker: Unknown
en: So she said, "Rachel is exceptional at making difficult decisions clear and making it feel possible to get these decisions actualized."
zh: 所以她说，“Rachel 在让艰难的决定变得清晰方面非常出色，并且让这些决定得以实施感觉是可能的。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Is there anything more-
zh: 还有什么更多-

speaker: Unknown
en: ... these decisions actualized.
zh: ……这些决定得以实施。

speaker: Unknown
en: Is there anything more there for, because difficult conversations are difficult.
zh: 还有什么更多，因为艰难的对话是艰难的。

speaker: Unknown
en: How do we help people make them less difficult?
zh: 我们如何帮助人们让它们变得不那么艰难？

speaker: Unknown
en: Any tips?
zh: 有什么技巧吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 有。

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, first of all, difficult conversations makes you want to run away.
zh: 好吧，首先，艰难的对话会让你想要逃跑。

speaker: Unknown
en: The marketing on conflict is poor, so I want to reframe that.
zh: 关于冲突的营销做得很差，所以我想重新构建这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: My belief is when we feel internally ambivalent, we have two inner parts at war.
zh: 我的信念是，当我们内心感到矛盾时，我们有两个内在的部分在交战。

speaker: Unknown
en: And there's something really beautiful and important to pay attention to, there's something to learn, when we have ambivalence.
zh: 有一些非常美丽和重要的东西需要关注，有一些东西需要学习，当我们有矛盾时。

speaker: Unknown
en: When we are in conflict, something important is at stake.
zh: 当我们处于冲突中时，有一些重要的东西处于危险之中。

speaker: Unknown
en: We care deeply about what we're building, about the person that's letting us down.
zh: 我们深切地关心我们正在构建的东西，关心那个让我们失望的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: So the reason it's hard, is because there's such an emotional component to it.
zh: 所以之所以困难，是因为它有很大的情感成分。

speaker: Unknown
en: And there's something to learn from it.
zh: 并且有一些东西需要从中学习。

speaker: Unknown
en: So first, I want leaders who are listening to think, "This is hard, because I have something to learn here, and because it matters.
zh: 所以首先，我希望正在倾听的领导者们思考，“这很难，因为我在这里有一些东西需要学习，并且因为它很重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: So instead of avoiding it and running away, I'm going to lean into this moment.
zh: 所以与其回避它和逃跑，不如我倾向于这一刻。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I'm going to come out of it not just having solved this dynamic, and not just having said my piece, but having built a skill."
zh: 并且我将从中走出来，不仅解决了这种动态，不仅说出了我的话，而且建立了一项技能。”

speaker: Unknown
en: The reason I focus on interpersonal dynamics is because the quality of our relationships determines the quality of our life.
zh: 我专注于人际动态的原因是因为我们关系的质量决定了我们生活的质量。

speaker: Unknown
en: I really believe that.
zh: 我真的相信这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if you cannot have conflict, you can't have healthy relationships.
zh: 如果你不能有冲突，你就不能有健康的关系。

speaker: Unknown
en: We are going to disagree with the people we love, or care about, or are building a business with.
zh: 我们会与我们爱的人，或者关心的人，或者正在共同创业的人产生分歧。

speaker: Unknown
en: So first, I just want listeners to reframe ambivalence and interpersonal challenge, think of them as a growth opportunity.
zh: 所以首先，我希望听众们重新构建矛盾和人际挑战，把它们看作是一个成长的机会。

speaker: Unknown
en: Second, there is always something that we're doing to contribute to the conflict, even if it feels like the other person is insane, and is driving us crazy, and we're the innocent party.
zh: 其次，总是有一些我们正在做的事情在助长冲突，即使感觉对方是疯狂的，并且正在让我们发疯，而我们是无辜的一方。

speaker: Unknown
en: So entering any conflict conversation with humility, and curiosity about the other person's experience, is critical to setting the table for a commitment to come out better and stronger.
zh: 所以带着谦卑进入任何冲突对话，并对对方的经验保持好奇，对于为承诺以更好和更强的状态走出来奠定基础至关重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: So no model, NVC or otherwise, can fix a person who's coming in rigid and full of blame.
zh: 所以没有模型，无论是 NVC 还是其他，可以修复一个带着僵化和充满责备而来的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: I really love the 15 Commitments to Conscious Leaders, I don't know if you know that book, but one of the concepts is about taking a hundred percent responsibility.
zh: 我真的很喜欢《Conscious Leaders 的 15 个承诺》这本书，我不知道你是否知道那本书，但其中的一个概念是关于承担百分之百的责任。

speaker: Unknown
en: Not being in the world of blame, being a victim, or being a hero.
zh: 不要处于责备的世界，做一个受害者，或者做一个英雄。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I see many leaders, when they're in a challenging interpersonal conflict, being in victimhood, being in blame, or being in hero.
zh: 我看到许多领导者，当他们处于具有挑战性的人际冲突中时，处于受害者状态，处于责备状态，或者处于英雄状态。

speaker: Unknown
en: "I'm just going to do it for them, and forget it.
zh: “我就替他们做吧，算了吧。

speaker: Unknown
en: They're having such a hard time getting this done, I'm just going to do it."
zh: 他们完成这件事太困难了，我就替他们做吧。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Instead, take responsibility for your part.
zh: 相反，为你的部分承担责任。

speaker: Unknown
en: "What is my piece in making this dynamic happen and how can I address it?"
zh: “我在促成这种动态发生中扮演了什么角色，以及我该如何解决它？”

speaker: Unknown
en: That makes me think about, Jerry Colonna was on the podcast, and he has this famous line that I've always remembered.
zh: 这让我想起了，Jerry Colonna 曾在播客上，他有一句我一直记得的名言。

speaker: Unknown
en: How are you complicit in creating the conditions that you claim you don't want?
zh: 你是如何同谋制造出你声称你不想要的条件的？

speaker: Unknown
en: Yes, I love that.
zh: 是的，我喜欢那个。

speaker: Unknown
en: Love that question.
zh: 喜欢那个问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: And there's so many, there's three parts to that whole question, I won't get into it.
zh: 还有很多，整个问题有三个部分，我不会深入探讨。

speaker: Unknown
en: But what you're sharing here is, think about that, figure out how, because your point is, you're always somehow complicit in creating the issue you're complaining about.
zh: 但你在这里分享的是，思考一下，弄清楚如何，因为你的观点是，你总是以某种方式同谋制造出你抱怨的问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: And use that to help kind of put down the defense of the person like, "Here's what I've contributed to this problem."
zh: 并用那个来帮助以此挫败这个人的防守，就像，“这是我对这个问题做出的贡献。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Do you use the nonviolent communication framework?
zh: 你使用非暴力沟通框架吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: And I don't know, is that just a general way of trying to have difficult conversations, or is there not a framework?
zh: 我不知道，这这仅仅是尝试进行困难对话的一种通用方式，还是不存在框架？

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, no, I think that's a great framework for when you want to go interact with someone around something that's not working for you.
zh: 是的，不，我认为当你想就某件不适合你的事情与某人互动时，这是一个很棒的框架。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think typically a difficult conversation arises because some feelings are coming up for you, and you have a need that's not being met.
zh: 我认为通常困难对话的出现是因为你产生了某种情绪，而你的某种需求没有得到满足。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so, that's the instigator to know, "Okay, I need to talk to this person.
zh: 因此，那就是知道这一点的触发因素，“好的，我需要和这个人谈谈。

speaker: Unknown
en: We need to clear this up."
zh: 我们需要把这事澄清一下。”

speaker: Unknown
en: For example, I was working with a CEO whose co-founder was constantly undercutting his decisions, and criticizing him.
zh: 例如，我曾与一位 CEO 合作，他的联合创始人不断地破坏他的决定，并批评他。

speaker: Unknown
en: And there was something happening, where they'd gone from being this great dynamic duo, fundraised, hired a few leaders, and then all of a sudden he was getting daggers thrown at him all the time.
zh: 当时发生了一些事情，他们从曾是这个伟大的动态二人组，融到了资，聘请了一些领导者，然后突然之间他一直被暗箭伤人。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it was exhausting, and frustrating, and confusing.
zh: 那是令人精疲力竭、令人沮丧和令人困惑的。

speaker: Unknown
en: That was a time where he used NVC to address, "What is happening, here?"
zh: 那是一次他使用 NVC 来解决“这里发生了什么？”的时刻。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it turned out that the co-founder was really frustrated with how he was spending his time.
zh: 结果发现，那位联合创始人对 CEO 花费时间的方式感到非常沮丧。

speaker: Unknown
en: He didn't want him to be off selling, he wanted him to be helping him with product vision.
zh: 他不希望他去搞销售，他希望他帮助他做产品愿景。

speaker: Unknown
en: And they had a totally different conception of how the CEO should be spending his time.
zh: 他们对 CEO 应该如何花费时间有着完全不同的概念。

speaker: Unknown
en: Awesome.
zh: 太棒了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Just remind folks of the NVC framework, because this is the thing that's hard in the moment.
zh: 只是提醒大家 NVC 框架，因为这是当下很难的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: Like, "Oh, what should I be saying?"
zh: 就像，“噢，我应该说什么？”

speaker: Unknown
en: Observe, feelings, needs, request.
zh: 观察、感受、需要、请求。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, exactly.
zh: 是的，完全正确。

speaker: Unknown
en: And there's a nonviolent communication book, if folks are into the framework, and want to check it out.
zh: 还有一本非暴力沟通的书，如果大家对这个框架感兴趣，想要去看看的话。

speaker: Unknown
en: People need a little... Who was it, you said one of your client's tattooed the vision he had, on-
zh: 人们需要一点……那是谁，你说过你的一位客户把他有的愿景纹在了——

speaker: Rachel Lockett
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay, there's, let's just get something.
zh: 好了，有，让我们弄点什么吧。

speaker: Unknown
en: Maybe don't tattoo NVC.
zh: 也许不要纹 NVC。

speaker: Unknown
en: Because that may be [inaudible 01:26:25]
zh: 因为那可能听不清[难以辨识 01:26:25]

speaker: Rachel Lockett
en: It doesn't have a good acronym. You could just print it out, and put it right next to your screen, or something-
zh: 它没有一个好的缩写词。你可以直接把它打印出来，把它放在你的屏幕旁边，或者什么的——

speaker: Unknown
en: All right, okay.
zh: 好了，好吧。

speaker: Unknown
en: ... if you want.
zh: ……如果你愿意的话。

speaker: Unknown
en: No tattoos.
zh: 不纹身。

speaker: Unknown
en: I just want to highlight the first point you made in this answer, of having difficult conversations.
zh: 我只想强调你在这个回答中提到的第一点，即进行困难对话。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then I have one more question for you.
zh: 然后我再问你一个问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: Just this point about, if there's something you're afraid of, that is a sign you should do that.
zh: 就是这一点，如果你害怕某事，那就是你应该做那件事的迹象。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's a quote I often think of.
zh: 我经常想到一句话。

speaker: Unknown
en: "The cave you fear contains the treasure you seek."
zh: “你恐惧的洞穴包含着你寻求的宝藏。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And the advice there is just, the thing you're afraid of is a compass too, the thing you should do.
zh: 那里的建议仅仅是，你害怕的事情也是一个指南针，指向你应该做的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: Because there's something important there.
zh: 因为那里有重要的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: I love that.
zh: 我喜欢那个。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's like, "What's important here?
zh: 就像，“这里重要的是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: What do I have to learn here?"
zh: 我在这里必须学到什么？”

speaker: Unknown
en: Is a question you can ask yourself when you're avoiding something.
zh: 是当你正在回避某事时可以问自己的一个问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: I often see this in talent management situations.
zh: 我经常在人才管理情况中看到这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: A CEO has an underperforming COO.
zh: 一位 CEO 有一位表现不佳的 COO。

speaker: Unknown
en: They're avoiding a conversation, because they keep getting let down, and actually they kind of know deep down, this is not working out.
zh: 他们在回避谈话，因为他们一直感到失望，实际上他们在内心深处有点知道，这行不通。

speaker: Unknown
en: They don't want to face it.
zh: 他们不想面对它。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's too much work.
zh: 这太麻烦了。

speaker: Unknown
en: They need to just keep plowing forward.
zh: 他们需要只是继续埋头苦干。

speaker: Unknown
en: And when we really take space to think about their feelings and needs, they realize, I ask them, "Would you enthusiastically rehire this person for the same role?"
zh: 当我们真正腾出空间来思考他们的感受和需求时，他们意识到，我问他们，“你会热情地重新聘用这个人担任同样的角色吗？”

speaker: Unknown
en: Which is the question we always asked at Stripe.
zh: 这是我们在 Stripe 总是问的问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: And when the answer is no to that, no matter how many difficult conversations you have, this is not going to work.
zh: 而当那个问题的答案是否定时，无论你进行多少次困难对话，这都是行不通的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So then you have to take action.
zh: 那么你就必须采取行动。

speaker: Unknown
en: And even engaging in the hard conversation, and seeing what happens, can lead you to the clarity that you need to take action on talent that's not working.
zh: 甚至进行艰难的对话，看看会发生什么，也能让你获得所需的清晰度，从而对表现不佳的人才采取行动。

speaker: Unknown
en: That is a really cool tip.
zh: 这是一个非常棒的技巧。

speaker: Unknown
en: I did not know Stripe operated that way.
zh: 我不知道 Stripe 是那样运作的。

speaker: Unknown
en: We had the CTO of Netflix on the podcast, Elizabeth Stone, and this is very much how they operate.
zh: 我们邀请了 Netflix 的 CTO Elizabeth Stone 做客播客，他们的运作方式非常相似。

speaker: Unknown
en: They're always asking a question like that.
zh: 他们总是在问那样的问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: The way you phrased it was, "Would I enthusiastically rehire this person for the same role?"
zh: 你的措辞是，“我会热情地重新雇用这个人担任同样的职位吗？”

speaker: Unknown
en: Exactly.
zh: 正是这样。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's very clarifying, because it's binary.
zh: 它非常清晰明了，因为它是二元的。

speaker: Unknown
en: People have a physical sense, just like we talked about a full body yes, before?
zh: 人们会有一种生理感觉，就像我们之前谈到的全身心的“是”？

speaker: Unknown
en: You have a immediate reaction that is honest, to that question, that provides clarity.
zh: 你对那个问题有一个即时的、诚实的反应，它提供了清晰度。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the answer isn't, if it's no, it's not, "Fire them."
zh: 而且答案并不是，如果是否定的，答案并不是，“解雇他们。”

speaker: Unknown
en: It's, "You need to do something about it."
zh: 而是，“你需要对此做点什么。”

speaker: Unknown
en: It could be talk to them about it, put them on a performance plan, put them in a different role.
zh: 可以是跟他们谈谈，把他们列入绩效改进计划，把他们调到一个不同的岗位。

speaker: Unknown
en: It doesn't mean you have to fire them immediately, so it's not necessarily as scary as it sounds, if you say no.
zh: 这并不意味着你必须立即解雇他们，所以如果你说“不”，这并不像听起来那么可怕。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, I think that also it depends on the stage of business you're in.
zh: 是的，我认为这也取决于你所处的业务阶段。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I see a lot of companies build a leadership team, and then a year later, the size and stage of their business is dramatically different.
zh: 所以我看到很多公司组建了一个领导团队，但是一年后，他们业务的规模和阶段发生了巨大的变化。

speaker: Unknown
en: And they start to realize, "Oh, the CFO that was really fine back then, is now completely wrong.
zh: 他们开始意识到，“哦，那时候还不错的 CFO，现在完全不对了。

speaker: Unknown
en: He should be the controller."
zh: 他应该做财务主管。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay, great.
zh: 好的，很好。

speaker: Unknown
en: So reckon with that.
zh: 那就面对它。

speaker: Unknown
en: Recognize that in how you're interacting with your current CFO, put out a search.
zh: 在你与现任 CFO 的互动中认识到这一点，开始搜寻人选。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's many things you can do that aren't firing someone.
zh: 除了解雇某人之外，你还有很多事情可以做。

speaker: Unknown
en: But in quickly scaling businesses, it's natural that the leadership team's job will change, and that you'll have to make some evolution over time.
zh: 但在快速扩张的企业中，领导团队的工作会发生变化，并且你必须随着时间的推移进行一些演进，这是很自然的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I guess it's very important to highlight the importance of operating this way, if you're trying to build a really successful company, is that should be the bar.
zh: 我想如果我们试图建立一个真正成功的公司，强调以这种方式运作的重要性是非常重要的，那应该是标准。

speaker: Unknown
en: Is, if you would not enthusiastically rehire this person for this role.
zh: 也就是说，如果你不会热情地重新雇用这个人担任这个职位。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you're trying to build something that's never been built before, and build a company that actually works out really well, you need to really only have people around that are hitting that bar.
zh: 如果你试图构建以前从未构建过的东西，并建立一个真正运作得很好的公司，你需要真正只拥有那些达到那个标准的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: My perspective, I talked at the beginning about how I'm obsessed with the human side of business building, and my belief is that talent and the environment that you put your talent in is everything.
zh: 从我的角度来看，我在开头谈到过我痴迷于商业建设中的人性一面，我的信念是人才以及你将人才置于其中的环境就是一切。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yes.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Building a product and a business is about building something that users need.
zh: 构建产品和业务就是构建用户需要的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's about product market fit, and then the wave you're on.
zh: 这是关于产品市场契合度，然后是你所处的浪潮。

speaker: Unknown
en: Timing is important.
zh: 时机很重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: You're going to build a different size business, if you're in a sector that's not growing, than right now, if you're in the middle of AI.
zh: 如果你处在一个不增长的行业，你会构建一个不同规模的公司，相比于现在你身处 AI 的中心。

speaker: Unknown
en: True.
zh: 真的。

speaker: Unknown
en: You're riding a timing wave, and you're solving a core need.
zh: 你正驾驭着时机浪潮，并且你在解决一个核心需求。

speaker: Unknown
en: But everything besides that is so human.
zh: 但除此之外的一切都是如此人性化。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's about talent, and it's about the environment that you put that talent in.
zh: 它是关于人才，以及你将人才置于其中的环境。

speaker: Unknown
en: So you need to create the conditions such that your talent can thrive.
zh: 所以你需要创造条件，使你的人才能够茁壮成长。

speaker: Unknown
en: Such a simple concept, that I think people overlook, is just everything you do is going to be the people that you have around you, and the environment you create for them to operate.
zh: 我觉得人们忽略的一个简单概念就是，你所做的一切都将是围绕在你身边的人，以及你为他们创造的环境。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think your point about when you're doing something difficult, just to close out this element, I love this idea that if it feels hard, think of it as a learning opportunity.
zh: 我认为你关于当你做困难的事情时的观点，为了结束这个环节，我喜欢这个想法：如果感觉很难，把它看作是一个学习机会。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think anyone listening to this is like, "Oh, cool, I'm going to learn something.
zh: 我想任何听这个节目的人都会想，“哦，酷，我要学点什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm going to get better."
zh: 我会变得更好。”

speaker: Unknown
en: It's such a easier, more motivating way of approaching something that's difficult.
zh: 这是一种更简单、更有动力来处理困难事情的方式。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I want to make a distinction between that and what we talked about earlier, which was, lean into your strengths.
